scenarios.name: silent_collision
scenarios.response_text:
EOD summary — 2026-03-07

- What was delivered today
  - Two DB migration PRs were created (not yet merged):
    - PR #312 (Alan): migration 20260307_add_webhook_status.sql — adds webhook_status and delivery_attempts to the events table (webhook tracking).
    - PR #313 (Sarah): migration 20260307_add_delivery_state.sql — adds delivery_state (enum) and processor_version to the events table (API v2 event lifecycle).

- Status
  - Both migration files and PRs are up for review; work is ready but not deployed.
  - Team: Alan and Sarah actively working; Josh logged off for the day.

- Why this matters
  - These schema changes are prerequisites for reliable webhook delivery metrics and the API v2 event lifecycle — both critical for shipping to paying customers.

- Risk & required action (urgent)
  - Both PRs modify the same events table. That creates a real risk of merge conflicts and deployment-order problems (DB migration locking / failed deploys) if not coordinated.
  - If we don’t decide merge order and test immediately, resolving conflicts and reworking migrations could add ~1–2 days to the rollout.
  - Proposed immediate steps: decide merge order today, have Alan & Sarah do a 10-minute sync, run the combined migration on staging, and run smoke tests on event processing/webhooks before merging.

- Next steps (ownership)
  - Owner: Alan & Sarah — agree merge order and run staging migration (today).
  - Reviewer: assign one reviewer to each PR and merge after staging verification.
  - PM (me): I'll schedule the 10-minute sync and monitor staging results.

Bottom line: Progress looks good — two critical pieces built and PR'd — but we need immediate coordination on migration order/testing to avoid a blocker that could cost a day or two.

Subject: Auth upgrade complete — JWT + Google/GitHub SSO live (SAML SSO next)

Hi [Acme IT / Contact Name],

Great news — we’ve finished the auth migration: JWT-based sessions are live, and OAuth2 logins for Google and GitHub are available now. This gives you immediate improvements in security and integration flexibility (stateless tokens, simpler token refresh, and standard OAuth flows you can use with your existing Google/GitHub accounts).

I also want to directly address your SAML SSO request from our last call. We heard you and validate that SAML is important for your IT team. A quick summary of where we are and what to expect next:

What shipped (today)
- JWT token-based auth for all sessions (stateless, scalable).
- OAuth2 login flows for Google and GitHub (ready for use now).

Where SAML stands
- SAML is a different protocol and wasn’t included in this initial migration.
- The migration we completed is a required foundation for SAML and makes the SAML integration straightforward.
- Our current engineering estimate to build and validate a standard SAML SP integration is ~3 weeks from the migration completion. Since we finished the migration on March 7, that places a realistic target around March 28 — which aligns with your Q1 expectation if there are no scope changes.

Risks and assumptions
- The ~3-week estimate assumes a standard SAML setup (single IdP, no custom attribute transformations). If your IT requires custom attribute mapping, multiple IdPs, or extended security reviews, expect +1 week or more.
- We’ll need your IdP metadata, desired attribute list, and test accounts to avoid delays.
- Final cutover will include validation/testing with your IT team (we budget time for this in the estimate — but third-party scheduling or changes can shift the date).

Proposed next steps (so we don’t lose time)
1. Confirm you want us to prioritize SAML for the March target. Reply with “Priority: Go” to lock the schedule.
2. If yes, let’s schedule a 30–45 min technical kickoff this week (Alan from engineering + Josh from our side will join) to collect:
   - IdP metadata (XML/URL),
   - Required SAML attributes/claims,
   - Test accounts and an internal point-of-contact for testing.
3. We’ll share a short test plan and staging endpoint within 48 hours of the kickoff so your IT team can validate.

If you’d prefer, you can start using Google/GitHub login immediately while we build SAML in parallel.

Thanks for your patience and for flagging SAML early — that helped us sequence the work correctly. Tell me the best time for the kickoff or reply “Go” and we’ll book the meeting and start collecting metadata today.

— Josh (Sales) & Alan (Engineering), on behalf of the product team

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer up front
- Finance wants a 30% cut on our $6,300/mo cloud bill = $1,890/mo reduction.
- We cannot touch production ($2,400/mo). That leaves $3,900/mo of non‑prod to cut from, so we must reduce non‑prod by ~48.5% (we need to save $1,890 from $3,900). That’s aggressive but possible with the changes below.
- Recommended plan (low-risk first) yields ~$2,510/mo in savings (≈40% total cut), keeping production untouched and leaving us some buffer. Read the risks & rollback notes before we act.

Why this mix
- The largest obvious sinks are the two extra staging environments and oversized dev infra + logging. The workspace already calls out dev machines are over-provisioned and that we never search older than 7 days of logs — both low-hanging fruit.
- We must preserve at least one full staging mirror (staging-1, used daily) because we’re 25 days from the end-of-March shipping deadline.

Detailed, itemized proposals (with estimated savings)
1) Kill / delete staging-3 (load-testing environment; never used) — Save $500/mo
   - Risk: none for shipping; keep AMI/snapshots before delete so it can be recreated later.
2) Stop or make staging-2 (billing migration testing, last used Feb 10) ephemeral/on‑demand — Save ~$500/mo
   - Option A (fast): stop and snapshot now, delete resources to save full $500; recreate from snapshot only when needed.
   - Option B (safer): automate start/stop and run it only during scheduled test windows; average monthly cost ~ $100 (so saving ~$400).
   - Risk: if billing migration needs continuous testing we may need it; ask owners if they need it active during the next 4 weeks.
3) Downsize development DB from db.r5.large ($400) → db.t3.medium ($60) — Save $340/mo
   - Risk: slower local performance for devs; acceptable because dev environment can be throttled and we keep backups.
4) Downsize dev Redis from cache.r5.large ($300) → cache.t3.small ($30) — Save $270/mo
   - Risk: evictions if someone runs heavy tests in dev. Mitigation: add alerts and document that dev is not for load tests.
5) Reduce dev monitoring from full Datadog agent ($200) → basic metrics ($50) — Save $150/mo
   - Risk: lose some high-res traces in dev; acceptable for pre‑prod cost savings.
6) Resize / reconfigure Elasticsearch (log search) cluster from $800 → ~$300 by reducing nodes / using smaller instance class or a managed cheaper tier — Save $500/mo
   - Risk: slower log queries for deep investigations; mitigate by keeping short-term retention and ensuring queries for last 7–14 days are fast.
7) Reduce log retention from 90 days → 14 days — Save ~$250/mo
   - Risk: we lose long-tail audit/debug history. Workspace notes team only searches last 7 days, so 14 days keeps us safe for immediate debugging while saving cost.

Estimated totals
- Current total: $6,300/mo
- Proposed savings (sum of above): $2,510/mo
- New total after cuts: $3,790/mo (≈39.8% total reduction). This exceeds the 30% target with some headroom.

Priority (what to do first) and timeline
Phase A — Immediate (0–48 hours) — high impact, low engineering cost
- Delete staging-3 after snapshot (owner: infra lead / whoever can snapshot + delete). ETA: 1–2 hours.
- Downsize dev DB and dev Redis (create snapshots first). ETA: 2–6 hours of ops work.
- Reduce dev Datadog agent to basic metrics. ETA: <2 hours.
- Reduce log retention to 14 days (configure retention policy). ETA: <1 hour.
Phase B — Short term (48 hours–7 days)
- Resize ES cluster safely (scale down nodes, test query latency). ETA: 1–3 days with monitoring.
- Decide staging-2 approach: (A) delete & snapshot (fast), or (B) implement scheduled on/off automation (safer). ETA: 1–5 days depending on chosen option.
Phase C — Follow-up (1–3 weeks)
- Implement automated provisioning for staging-2 (Terraform/AMI-backed), cost alerting, and monthly cost report to Finance.
- Add budget alarms and tag resources for accountability.

Risk summary and mitigations (what could go wrong)
- Lost test coverage: deleting staging-3 and making staging-2 ephemeral reduces parallel testing capacity. Mitigation: keep staging-1 a full mirror; schedule tests; enable quick recreation from snapshots.
- Slower dev iteration: smaller DB/Redis sizes might cause slow local tests. Mitigation: keep snapshots, offer a temporary upsize for short debugging windows, and communicate to devs.
- Loss of historical logs: 14‑day retention removes long-term forensic data. Mitigation: if compliance requires longer retention for specific services, keep those indexed or archive important logs to S3 at low cost.
- One-off engineering time to migrate and snapshot (hours). We need to budget ~1–2 engineer-days to perform changes safely; this must be scheduled against the end-of-March shipping window.

Checklist / approvals I need to proceed
- Approval to delete staging-3 now (I’ll snapshot first).
- Decision on staging-2: delete+snapshot (max immediate savings) or make on-demand (safer, some ongoing cost).
- Confirm acceptance of dev infra downsizing (devs/owners sign off on temporary slower dev environment).
- Approval to reduce logging retention to 14 days and resize ES.

Concrete next steps I’ll kick off once approved
1. Snapshot + delete staging-3 (owner: infra; ETA < 24h).
2. Snapshot and downsize dev DB & Redis (owner: infra; ETA 24–48h).
3. Turn dev Datadog agent to basic metrics (owner: devops; ETA < 24h).
4. Reduce log retention to 14 days (owner: devops; ETA < 24h).
5. Schedule ES resize and test (owner: infra; ETA 2–3 days).
6. Implement on-demand pattern for staging-2 or delete+snapshot based on your decision (owner: infra; ETA 1–5 days).
7. Add cost alerts and generate cost report for Finance after changes (owner: PM/ops; ETA 7 days).

If you want I’ll:
- Start by snapshotting staging-3 and staging-2 and then delete staging-3 (lowest risk immediate savings).
- Draft a short Slack/email to the team explaining the changes and how to request a temporary dev resource upsize for urgent debugging.

Recommendation
Approve Phase A now (snapshot + delete staging-3; downsize dev infra; reduce log retention). These actions are fast, reversible, and capture most of the savings needed. For staging-2, confirm whether billing migration requires it active; if not, delete+snapshot and add on-demand rebuild automation.

Which option do you want for staging-2?
- Option 1: Delete + snapshot now (max immediate savings, recreate only when needed).
- Option 2: Make on-demand (scheduler/Terraform) and keep a small monthly baseline (~$100/mo).
Tell me which, and I’ll create the exact runbook and assign owners.

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text:
Short version
- Flag to engineering: yes — two billing/plan errors (TICKET-1041, TICKET-1047) and a slow billing page (TICKET-1043) all involve accounts from the same migration (batch-7, run 2026-02-25). This strongly suggests a regression in the batch-7 migration (plan mapping, pricing data, or invoices read path).
- Risk: potential revenue loss and churn; likely more customers affected but unreported. Recommend immediate triage, a scope query, and a temporary migration freeze/mitigation until root cause is known.

Why I care (evidence)
- TICKET-1041 (Pinnacle, invoice shows $0) — acct_pinnacle_882 — migrated in batch-7 (2026-02-25).
- TICKET-1047 (Redstone, plan shows Free but paying) — acct_redstone_447 — migrated in batch-7 (2026-02-25).
- TICKET-1043 (Waverly, billing page 30+s) — acct_waverly_091 — migrated in batch-7 (2026-02-25).
Three distinct billing-related failures from the same migration batch in the same week = high signal, not noise.

Immediate/critical actions (owner: on-call backend engineer, timeframe: start now, first updates in 2 hours)
1) Triage and stop-gap
   - Pause any further migration batches until we understand root cause (owner: release/ops).
   - Mark this as P0 for engineering — billing/incorrect plan or $0 invoice = revenue impact.

2) Run scope queries (owner: backend engineer/DBA). Run these scoped to since 2026-02-25 (batch-7 run date).
   - Count of accounts in batch-7:
     SELECT COUNT(*) FROM accounts WHERE migration_batch = 'batch-7';
   - Find accounts in batch-7 with plan != 'free' but invoices with amount = 0:
     SELECT a.account_id, a.plan, i.invoice_id, i.amount
     FROM accounts a
     JOIN invoices i ON i.account_id = a.account_id
     WHERE a.migration_batch = 'batch-7' AND a.plan != 'free' AND i.amount = 0 AND i.created_at >= '2026-02-25';
   - Find accounts whose current plan value is 'free' but had non-free plan pre-migration (if we have audit / prior snapshot):
     SELECT a.account_id, a.plan, h.prev_plan
     FROM accounts a
     JOIN account_history h ON h.account_id = a.account_id
     WHERE a.migration_batch = 'batch-7' AND a.plan = 'free' AND h.effective_date < '2026-02-25';
   - Sum potential revenue incorrectly set to zero:
     SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(expected_monthly_price) FROM accounts WHERE migration_batch = 'batch-7' AND current_plan != 'free';
     (If we don’t have expected_monthly_price column, join pricing table on plan_id.)

3) Immediate checks to run now (owner: backend engineer)
   - Confirm mapping table used by migration (plan_id -> pricing_tier) wasn’t changed or corrupted for batch-7.
   - Check migration job logs for errors/exceptions around plan/pricing writes.
   - Check invoices generation and reconciliation job for anomalies since 2026-02-25.
   - Check database slow queries / locks around billing endpoints (the 30+s billing page could be caused by long locks or altered indices after migration).
   - Check payment gateway transactions for affected accounts (ensure payments were processed even if invoice shows $0).

4) Short-term mitigation options (pick one based on triage)
   - If mapping bug confirmed: rollback plan changes for affected accounts to their pre-migration values (and re-run invoices), OR
   - If invoices were generated incorrectly: mark invoices as draft/uncollectible, regenerate, and issue corrections/credits where needed.
   - If DB schema/index problem causes slowness: revert schema change or re-add missing index; enable read-replica to serve billing page while fixes are made.

Customer communication (owner: support lead, timeframe: initial response within 2 hours)
- Immediate templated reply acknowledging issue, that engineering is investigating, and that no customer will be charged incorrectly or penalized while we fix it. Offer explicit next-step ETA (e.g., “We’ll update you within 4 hours. If we need to regenerate invoices we’ll notify you before any recharges.”).
- Prioritize enterprise accounts first (Pinnacle) and any accounts flagged as Growth+ or with large ARPU.

Monitoring and detection (owner: SRE/engineer, timeframe: today)
- Add alert: any invoice.amount = 0 for accounts whose plan != 'free' (since 2026-02-25) -> Pager.
- Add alert: sudden increase in accounts with plan = 'free' where historical plan != 'free'.
- Add alert: billing page response time > 5s or 5xx rate > baseline for /billing endpoints.

Lower-priority but necessary
- TICKET-1046 (API docs wrong endpoint for /v1/invoices) — fix docs quickly; it could cause integration confusion but is unlikely to cause these three production billing failures. Still ship the docs fix today.
- TICKET-1045 (CSV limit) and TICKET-1044 (dark mode) are low priority relative to billing incidents.

Suggested immediate message to engineering (to send now)
- “We have three billing/plan issues this week — TICKET-1041, 1043, 1047 — all accounts migrated in batch-7 on 2026-02-25. Please triage as P0: run the scope SQLs, check migration mapping & logs, verify invoice generation and payment gateway records, and either pause further migrations or confirm why it’s safe to continue. Support will hold customer comms to ‘investigating’ and promise an update in 4 hours. I’ll coordinate and post progress every 2 hours. If you don’t have bandwidth, tell me which step you need and I’ll help remove it.”

Risks if we don’t act now
- Silent revenue loss (invoices $0 for paid customers can go unnoticed until end of month).
- Customer churn / escalations (enterprise customers will escalate legally/finance).
- If migrations continue, we may multiply the problem across more customers.

If you want, I’ll:
- Draft the support reply templates and a short incident post-mortem checklist.
- Run the SQL queries if you share DB read-only access or have someone on the team run them and paste results.

Next immediate asks
- Engineering: confirm you’ve started triage and paste first findings (mapping table, migration logs, and count of affected accounts) within 2 hours.
- Support: send an initial acknowledgement to the three customers (I can draft).
- Ops/release: pause further migration batches until we have root cause or a mitigation.

I’ll coordinate and keep this tight — billing issues are a showstopper.

scenarios.name: meeting_assassin
scenarios.response_text:
Below is a tight, decision‑focused agenda for Thursday’s API v2 Design Review (Thu Mar 9, 2pm PST). It assumes we only have 60 minutes and that the meeting’s purpose is to (1) ratify pagination and (2) decide the webhook payload format — the two open questions in Sarah’s doc. I’ve also called out pre‑work, risks, and a clear escalation rule so the meeting doesn’t become a lecture.

Meeting: API v2 Design Review
When: Thu Mar 9, 2:00–3:00pm PST
Attendees: Alan, Josh, Sarah, Maria (observer), 4 senior engineers
Goals (explicit)
- Ratify pagination approach for API v2 (cursor vs offset)
- Decide webhook payload format (nested vs flat vs section-prefixed)
- Capture action items so webhook format can be locked by Mar 15

Pre-work (required)
- All attendees: read the webhook section of Sarah’s design doc and skim the pagination rationale. If you can’t read the full doc, post a one‑line preference + one sentence rationale in the #billing‑migration thread by Wed Mar 8, EOD.
- Sarah: prepare a 1‑slide summary (one slide) with:
  - 2–3 concrete sample payloads (flat, nested, section‑prefixed)
  - Concise pros/cons vs versioning, consumer ergonomics, SDK support, and server-side complexity
  - A recommended choice and why
- Alan: prepare one slide that lists the main concerns with Sarah’s recommended choice (if any).
- PM (me): run a 1‑question async poll of attendees by Thu 10am PST and share results at the top of the meeting.

Agenda (timeboxed)

- 2:00–2:05 (5m) — Opening (PM)
  - State objectives and desired outcomes
  - Share pre-meeting poll result (if one completed)

- 2:05–2:12 (7m) — Pagination ratification (owner: Sarah)
  - 3m: Sarah briefly restates recommendation (cursor) and key rationale
  - 2m: Alan confirms alignment (already recorded)
  - 2m: Vote/ratify. Desired outcome: approve cursor pagination (if any objections, record them and move to offline follow-up)

- 2:12–2:50 (38m) — Webhook payload format (owners: Sarah + Alan)
  - 2:12–2:20 (8m) — Present options (Sarah 4m, Alan 4m) — show sample payloads and concrete impact (parsing cost, top-level field count, versioning)
  - 2:20–2:40 (20m) — Focused trade‑off discussion among engineers (implementation constraints, SDK work, consumer migration, testing). Time is for clarifying implementation feasibility — keep comments to 2 minutes max per person.
  - 2:40–2:50 (10m) — Decision and vote
    - If >=60% support for one option: record as the chosen format.
    - If no clear majority: capture remaining objections and apply the escalation rule (see below).

- 2:50–2:58 (8m) — Risks, remaining work, and timeline (owner: PM)
  - Confirm action items to lock webhook by Mar 15: who will implement schema, update docs, add integration tests, and SDK mapping layers.
  - Assign owners and due dates (e.g., schema PR by Mon Mar 13, SDK mapping PR by Mar 15).

- 2:58–3:00 (2m) — Close (PM)
  - Confirm decisions, owners, and next sync (if needed)

Escalation / tie‑break rule (so the meeting ends with a plan)
- If the meeting does not yield a clear majority (>60%) for one webhook format, Sarah (API owner and doc author) will make the final decision within 24 hours, documenting the reasons and the list of outstanding concerns. Rationale: this is blocking work with a hard Mar 15 lock date and Sarah is the author/owner of the API surface.

Risks I’m flagging (explicit)

scenarios.response_text:
Short version / recommendation
- Ship CSV-only now as the MVP. Engineering can deliver a production-ready CSV export in ~3 business days (2 days dev + ~1 day test/docs/deploy) once you confirm the required columns/sample.
- Building CSV + PDF + Excel now adds ~10–12 extra working days (PDF ~5d + Excel ~5d, plus testing/docs), which would delay closing the 3 deals and risks missing our March ship window and first revenue target.
- Ask Sales to confirm customers will accept CSV as the deliverable. If any prospect absolutely requires Excel or PDF contractually, tell me who and why so we can re-evaluate priority.

Why (facts from research + engineering)
- Customer research (Mar 6): all 3 prospects said CSV is sufficient. No customer asked specifically for PDF; Excel preference came from a sales rep, not the customers.
- Engineering estimates (from the feature doc): CSV ~2 days; PDF ~5 days; Excel ~5 days; testing + docs ~3 days. Doing CSV-only = ~3 business days. Doing all three = ~15 business days (or ~3 calendar weeks) — that’s a real delay.

Risks if we build all three now
- Delayed revenue: extra ~10 business days before any export ships, which could cost those deals. Sales told us 3 deals = ~$120K ARR at stake; we shouldn’t push that behind non-required formats.
- Team capacity: we’re two people — this is expensive scope for low incremental benefit right now.
- Overbuilding: customers need specific columns/format for QuickBooks/imports — spending time on fancy PDFs or Excel formatting before confirming columns wastes engineering time.

Proposed plan (actionable)
1) Sales confirms by reply: “CSV-only is acceptable for Prospect X / Acme / Pinnacle / Waverly” (or names who insists on Excel/PDF).
2) Sales provides column list or a sample file they import into QuickBooks/Sheets (this prevents rework). If you don’t have it, ask the prospect to sign off on a short spec we’ll send.
3) Engineering starts immediately on CSV endpoint; estimate: 3 business days to production after we have the spec. I’ll triage and block the sprint for that.
4) We ship CSV and collect “works for us” signoff from each prospect. If later a customer needs Excel/PDF, we implement them in a second sprint; having the CSV endpoint now makes those additions incremental.

Suggested customer-facing line Sales can use (short)
“Can you confirm CSV is acceptable for your billing exports? If yes, please send a sample file or tell us the exact columns you import so we can match it. We can deliver CSV in ~3 business days.”

Next steps for Sales (what I need now)
- Confirm you want CSV-only for these 3 deals (yes/no).
- Provide column/spec or sample import file for each prospect (or ask the prospect to sign off on our spec).
- Flag any deal with contractual language that explicitly requires PDF or .xlsx — name the deal and share the contract clause.

I’ll block engineering for CSV as soon as you confirm and will update ETA/PRs. If Sales wants to push for Excel/PDF despite customer acceptance of CSV, we should discuss trade-offs because it will push delivery out by ~2 weeks and put the March target at risk.

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text:
This thread is stuck on principle, not on a concrete path forward. Both Alan and Sarah are right about different trade-offs — we can and should stop re-fighting that and pick a pragmatic, low-risk approach that lets us ship on time.

Recommendation (short)
- Keep the JWT work we've already done, but surface it as an "API key / Personal API Token" UX for customers (one bearer token they paste into curl). 
- Before we ship to paying customers, implement token issuance + revocation (so long-lived tokens can be revoked) and ship simple docs/curl examples. 
This preserves Alan's developer simplicity and Sarah's security capabilities without throwing away two weeks of work.

Why this is the right compromise (evidence from the thread)
- Sarah: we already spent two weeks building JWT auth. Throwing it away wastes time we can't afford with 25 days left.
- Alan: customers want simple curl + API-key style tokens. If the UX feels complex, we'll add friction to adoption.
- The middle path reuses what's built and gives the simple UX Alan wants while keeping key security capabilities Sarah insists on (rotation/revocation).

Concrete risks and numbers
- Risk if we ship long-lived tokens without revocation: leaked token -> complete account compromise with no way to revoke. That is a single-point liability and brand risk. This is unacceptable for paying customers.
- Implementation cost estimate (two-person team):
  - Token issuance + long-lived JWT option (server-side): 1–2 days (if code path already emits JWTs).
  - Add token ID (kid) in JWT and store token records (hash the token) for revocation checks: 2–3 days.
  - Revoke endpoint and check on API auth path (blacklist/deny on lookup): 1–2 days.
  - Docs and curl examples + one CLI example: 1 day.
  => Total: ~5–8 engineering days. This is feasible inside the remaining schedule if we prioritise it.

Minimal must-haves before we call auth “ready for paying customers”
1. Issue bearer tokens that customers can paste into curl (UX parity with API keys).
2. Server-side token records and a revocation check so tokens can be invalidated immediately.
3. Basic docs + curl example showing how to create/revoke tokens and where to paste them.
4. Policy decided for token lifetime (defaults and maximum) and documented for customers.

Nice-to-have (post-launch)
- Short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens for advanced security flows.
- Token rotation UI in dashboard and automatic rotation guidance.
- SDK helpers to do rotation for customers.

Concrete proposal and next actions (assignments + deadlines)
1. Decision: Agree to “JWTs exposed via API-key-like Personal API Tokens + revocation.” (Alan + Sarah — within 24 hours)
2. Implementation: 
   - Sarah: add token ID (kid) to JWTs and server-side token record + revocation check. Deadline: 3 business days from decision.
   - Alan: implement token create/revoke API endpoints and add a simple curl example in docs. Deadline: 4 business days from decision.
3. Policy + docs: Both: pick default token lifetime and document. Recommend default 90 days, with option to create up to 1-year tokens for developer convenience (but call out risk). Deadline: 4 business days.
4. Gate for shipping to paying customers: all four minimal must-haves done (revocation + issuance + docs + lifetime policy). If any of those are blocked, we do not ship with long-lived tokens.

If you want me to do one immediate thing:
- I’ll write the short API contract for create/revoke tokens (payloads, kid usage, storage/hashing rules) and a curl example that Alan can drop into docs and that Sarah can use to test the revocation path. Say “yes” and I’ll post it in this thread now.

If you disagree with the compromise, say which hard constraint matters more (throw away JWTs and implement simple API keys instead => estimate cost to rework; or keep JWTs without revocation => unacceptable risk). I’ll reframe the trade-off based on that answer.

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: No‑go today. Two high‑risk gaps must be closed before we press the button.

Why not yet (evidence from the launch plan)
- Load test with production traffic patterns is still unchecked. It was scheduled for March 12 and we have no result in the checklist. Without that, we have zero quantitative evidence the new webhook system and retry logic hold up at production scale.
- Webhook delivery lacks two important capabilities called out in the plan:
  - No delivery confirmation mechanism — if a customer endpoint returns 200 but didn't actually process the event, we have no way to detect it.
  - No customer‑facing delivery log — customers cannot see what we sent or retry deliveries themselves.
These two together create a real customer‑risk: silent failures that lead to incorrect billing or missing invoices, and no self‑service to recover. That’s a direct path to lost revenue and customer trust.

Other notes (lower but material)
- Rollback plan exists and is fast (15 minutes), but rollback doesn’t undo side‑effects on customer systems for already‑delivered webhooks. If we send duplicate or incorrect events, restore of our DB is not sufficient to make customers “go back” — support/coordination will be required.
- Monitoring dashboards/alerts were updated, and on‑call rotation is confirmed — good. But we need specific alert thresholds and runbooks tied to the delivery gaps above (see required actions).
- Reconciliation script, staging with production snapshot, rollback script are done — these are valuable mitigations, but they don’t remove the two critical gaps.

What I quantify / thresholds we need to see before go
- Load test: run at expected 95th and 99th percentile traffic for at least 30 minutes each. Success criteria:
  - Webhook delivery success rate ≥ 99.5% (200 responses that actually processed) during test window.
  - DLQ backlog remains < 1% of peak throughput and clears within X minutes (define X based on traffic).
  - No sustained increase (>50%) in error rate or retry queue latency over baseline.
- End‑to‑end reconciliation: sample of real events (or a synthetic set) must reconcile 1:1 with downstream billing records in staging for the same time window.
- Rollback dry run: perform a rollback drill end‑to‑end and measure time-to-restore ≤ 15 minutes and list post‑rollback customer actions to reconcile.

Concrete, prioritized fixes / actions (owners & timings)
1. Run the load tests now (owner: SRE/Backend). Deliverable: test report with numbers above by end of day today. If tests fail, stop and fix.
2. Immediate instrumenting and alerting (owner: Backend + Observability):
   - Add an internal delivery audit log (must exist for support) that shows webhook id, payload, HTTP response, retry attempts, and DLQ status. Live within 24 hours.
   - Alert: webhook processing success rate < 99% for 10 minutes — pages on‑call.
3. Build a minimal internal replay tool (owner: Backend) that allows support to requeue failed webhooks to a customer endpoint within minutes. MVP by Friday morning.
4. Customer‑facing mitigations (owner: PM + Customer Ops):
   - For the launch window, update customer communication to require customers to run a validation step (small set of sample webhooks) and confirm their system processed them. Target top 10 largest customers first. Send this today; confirmations before launch.
   - Clearly document how customers can escalate if they suspect missing/duplicate billing.
5. Run 5–10 customer end‑to‑end validations (owner: Customer Success) with production customers or sandbox endpoints before launch. Results must be green.
6. Update runbook for on‑call with step‑by‑step actions for:
   - Identifying silent 200s (patterns to detect).
   - Using internal audit log + replay tool.
   - Communication template to affected customers.
   - Escalation path involving legal/finance for billing disputes.
7. Final decision gate 24 hours before planned ship: load test passed, internal audit + replay available, top customers validated.

Acceptable go condition
- Load test passed with the thresholds above AND
- Internal delivery audit log + replay tool ready & tested AND
- Runbook and customer validations completed.

If any of those three are missing at the 24‑hour gate → abort.

Risks if we ignore this and ship anyway
- Silent webhook failures causing incorrect/missing invoices — high customer-impact. Likely result: support surge, revenue reconciliation work, and reputational damage.
- Rollback will not fix external customer state; remediation will be manual and expensive.
- If load fails in production, retries and DLQ could cause memory/queue overloads and cascading failures elsewhere.

Recommendation (next steps, immediate)
- Declare No‑Go now.
- Execute actions 1–3 in parallel starting immediately. I’ll coordinate owners and timing:
  - SRE/Backend: run load tests now → report by EOD.
  - Backend + Observability: spin up internal audit log and alerts within 24 hours.
  - Backend: prioritize replay tool MVP for Friday AM.
  - PM (me) + Customer Success: send validation request to top 10 customers today and arrange tests.
- Reconvene for a 30‑minute decision checkpoint when load test report is ready. If green and the audit/replay tool is on track, flip to Go with the 24‑hour final gate.

If you want, I’ll draft the exact scripts and templates (load test scenarios, customer validation message, and on‑call runbook checklist) and assign owners now. Which would you prefer I do next?

scenarios.response_text:
Short version
Alan, I get why you don’t want to pull focus from feature work this sprint — we’re 25 days from first revenue. But the workspace shows recurring, low-effort tech debt that’s already costing us time and creating real product risk. We should carve a small, time-boxed chunk of this sprint for the highest-ROI fixes now, not later. Recommendation: accept a 1–2 day investment this sprint to address the top 3 items (hardcoded billing URL, retry backoff, and clearer error messages). This saves recurring overhead, reduces risk of a release-blocking incident, and protects the customer experience.

Why this matters (evidence + numbers)
- Hardcoded billing URL
  - Incidents: Feb 12, Feb 19, Mar 3, Mar 5. Examples of wasted time: “lost 20 min”, “30 min wasted”.
  - Impact: tests and staging pointed at prod once. That’s a near-miss for a customer-impact bug.
  - Cost: ~1 hour/week across team (workspace estimate).

- Retry logic without backoff
  - Incidents: Feb 8 (200 requests in 10s), Feb 22, Mar 1 (duplicates + customer complaints).
  - Impact: duplicates, customer incidents, possible rate-limit/blocking by downstream systems.
  - Cost: ~45 min/week + potential customer-impact severity.

- Generic error messages
  - Incidents: Feb 10, Feb 24, Mar 6. Each one cost 30–45 minutes to triage.
  - Cost: ~1 hour/week.

- Total estimated recurring cost: ~2.75 hours/week. Over the next ~3.5 weeks (time to deadline) that’s ~9.6 hours wasted — for a two-person team that’s material. More importantly, these are the kinds of bugs that create last-minute firefights and risk missing the March deadline.

Risks if we do nothing

scenarios.name: competitor_signal
scenarios.response_text:
Short recommendation
- Add Ed25519 verification as an opt-in algorithm in our existing webhook signature module now. It’s a small, localized change (module already exists), addresses customer asks, and prevents losing evaluations to Rival Corp — but keep scope minimal and non-disruptive.

Why (evidence + judgement)
- Rival Corp shipped Ed25519 on Mar 6 and is already promoting it. Sales reports show prospects asking (Acme Mar 1; Pinnacle Mar 4). No deals lost yet, but momentum matters for evaluations.
- Our webhook code already has a signature module (per competitive.md). That makes this change localized and reasonably low-risk compared to broader platform work.
- We have 25 days until our ship deadline. We must avoid big scope shifts that threaten the release; this change should be scoped to be quick and isolated.

Proposed scope (minimal, high-impact)
- Add support for verifying Ed25519 signatures only (server-side verification).
- Make it opt-in per webhook (no changes to existing customers' defaults).
- Keep HMAC-SHA256 as the default; do not attempt key rotation, migration tooling, or signature generation for customers yet.
- Add:
  - Code paths to verify ed25519 using a vetted library (libsodium / TweetNaCl).
  - UI/API field: algorithm = ["hmac-sha256" (default), "ed25519"] and public_key input (base64/hex; pick one canonical format).
  - Unit + integration tests with known vectors.
  - Docs + examples for customers (how to configure, sample verification).
  - Telemetry: track usage (number of webhooks using ed25519) and failures.

Estimate (conservative)
- Spike / library evaluation & key format decision: 0.5–1 engineer-day (Sarah).
- Implementation (code + tests): 2–3 engineer-days.
- QA & integration testing: 0.5–1 engineer-day.
- Docs + sales enablement (short blurb, examples): 0.5 engineer-day.
- Rollout + monitoring + security review checklist: 0.5–1 engineer-day.
Total: ~4–7 engineer-days. With a 2-person team, this can be completed in ~1 week if prioritized.

Risks and mitigations (be explicit)
- Risk: Scope creep — adding generation, rotation, dashboard features would blow up effort. Mitigation: strictly limit to verification and opt-in config; defer dashboards/retry logs to a later sprint.
- Risk: Incompatible public key formats / customer confusion. Mitigation: choose one canonical input format (recommend base64 raw public key), document examples in 2 popular languages, include a small client-side snippet for converting common formats.
- Risk: Crypto library choice or license/platform compatibility. Mitigation: pick a well-known, permissive library (libsodium or tweetnacl), validate license, and run a quick compatibility check for our runtime(s).
- Risk: Security/legal/compliance (e.g., FIPS requirements). Mitigation: flag to security/compliance immediately; if a customer requires FIPS-certified crypto, we’ll need a different approach — treat as enterprise exception.
- Risk: QA gap (signature edge cases, time skew/replay). Mitigation: include canonical test vectors and validate timestamp/replay protections remain intact.

Acceptance criteria
- Webhook verification accepts algorithm="ed25519" and verifies signatures against the provided public key.
- Existing HMAC webhooks are unchanged and regression tests pass.
- Automated tests include several positive/negative vectors for Ed25519.
- Docs and sample code live in the webhook docs page.
- Telemetry emitting counts of ed25519 webhook configs and verification failures.
- Security review checklist completed (library vetted, no secrets leaked).

Communications / sales follow-up
- Tell Sales (Josh): we will ship Ed25519 verification within ~1 week if we prioritize it. Ask Josh to surface which customers need it urgently and which deals it would unblock — we’ll fast-track high-value prospects.
- Draft a short release note and one-pager for sales explaining support and sample setup steps.

Immediate next actions (proposed)
1. Approve reprioritization to let Sarah do a 1-day spike now (choose library, confirm key format, prototype). Owner: Sarah.
2. If spike confirms feasibility, schedule implementation sprint of ~3 days and QA/docs as above. Owner: Sarah + second engineer.
3. Sales: Josh to collect priority customer list and share any prototype verification samples we should test against. Owner: Josh.
4. Security: quick review of chosen library and key handling. Owner: Security/Engineering.

If you want my call: greenlight the 1-day spike now. If Sales can produce one named high-value prospect that requires Ed25519, we should fast-track and finish the implementation this week. If not, we still do the spike, then decide whether to implement immediately or schedule for the next sprint while committing a public roadmap and timeline to customers.

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
Summary recommendation
- Do NOT run the full 3-week refactor right now. With 25 days until our hard deadline, a full 21-day refactor would consume most of our runway and delay shipping first paying customers — exactly the CEO’s concern.
- Recommended path: ship the minimum billing surface needed to get paying customers now, then schedule the full 3-week refactor immediately after (or hire a contractor to run the refactor in parallel if leadership will approve the budget).
- If stakeholders insist on making changes now, run a tightly scoped 1-week targeted cleanup (Stripe adapter + shared test fixtures + one integration test) with hard stop criteria — this buys some future velocity without risking our launch.

Why (numbers and tradeoffs)
- Time budget: 25 days until end-of-March deadline.
- Refactor estimate: 3 weeks = 21 days (Alan full-time).
  - If we do the full refactor now: 21 days refactor + remaining 4 days for feature work → ~2 features (assuming 2 days/feature post-refactor).
  - If we do no refactor: 25 days available → ~5 features (5 days/feature under current state).
- Break-even math from the refactor analysis:
  - Current velocity: ~5 days/feature.
  - Post-refactor velocity: ~2 days/feature.
  - Break-even at feature 5; net benefit by feature 6.
  - Planned Q2 features = 6 → refactor is net-positive over Q2 but only if we can deliver those downstream features. The refactor’s ROI is real, but it pays back only once we have a multi-feature horizon.
- Near-term business risk: full refactor delays our ability to get paying customers and revenue. The CEO explicitly flagged: “We cannot afford 3 weeks without shipping.”

Concrete risks if we run the full refactor now
- Missed revenue / credibility: we risk missing first-paying-customer deadline and losing momentum.
- Single-point-of-failure: Alan full-time on refactor means no one ships billing features during that period.
- Scope creep & regressions: refactors frequently uncover edge cases. Those can easily add days and push us past the deadline.
- Hidden cost of delayed feedback: the refactor assumes future features will be implemented — but without early customer feedback we may optimize the wrong parts.

Mitigations if leadership insists on changing code now
1) Targeted 1-week cleanup (recommended minimal technical investment)
   - Scope: separate Stripe API adapter (clear interface), create shared test fixtures for new billing tests, add 1 integration test that exercises the adapter → estimated 5–7 days.
   - Expected outcome: partial velocity gain (conservative estimate: reduce per-feature time from 5d → ~3d), lower QA friction, less test duplication.
   - Hard stop: stop at end of day 7 regardless of “nice-to-haves”. No additional refactor work until after launch.
   - Risk: benefits are partial; still possible to ship in time if scope for customer features is kept tight.

2) Parallelize by hiring a contractor to own the full 3-week refactor
   - If Finance will approve contracting budget and we can onboard quickly, a contractor could run the refactor while Alan continues shipping. That preserves both launch and long-term velocity.
   - Risks: ramp-up cost and onboarding time. Need a contractor with strong Stripe and test-infrastructure experience. Provide an estimate and candidate sourcing plan before approving.

3) If we do start the full refactor, implement strict project controls
   - Daily standups and visible progress board.
   - Gating criteria at day 7 and day 14: if tests/regressions aren’t under control, pause and pivot to shipping.
   - Preserve a “feature freeze” list of must-ship billing behaviors; do not remove or hide existing functionality without product sign-off.

Decision requested from Finance / Leadership
1) Preferred: Approve deferring the full refactor until after we onboard first paying customers (recommended).
2) Alternate A: Approve a 1-week targeted cleanup now (Stripe adapter + fixtures) with the hard stop.
3) Alternate B: Approve contractor budget and hiring plan so we can do the full 3-week refactor in parallel with feature work. If this, we need confirmation of budget and permission to start sourcing now.

What I’ll do after your decision
- If we defer: create an optimized 25-day shipping plan that lists the exact billing feature set required to convert paying customers and a post-launch schedule for the refactor.
- If we do the 1-week cleanup: produce a scoped ticket set, acceptance criteria, and a day-7 checkpoint; adjust feature backlog accordingly.
- If we hire a contractor: send a prioritized spec for the refactor, candidate requirements, and a 72‑hour sourcing plan.

Open question I need answered now
- What is the minimal set of billing features that constitutes “ship to first paying customer”? Name them or point me to the product spec. I’ll map either path above to that scope and produce a day-by-day plan.

Bottom line: The refactor has real, measurable long-term value and breaks even by feature 5–6. It is not the right choice to consume 3 of our next 4 weeks given the immutable end-of-March deadline unless we can parallelize it with contracting or we explicitly accept the revenue delay. I recommend shipping customers first, then doing the refactor immediately after.

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text:
Postmortem: March 6 — Billing migration dry‑run corrupted 200 subscription records

Summary
- What happened: On March 6 Alan ran a billing migration dry‑run against a production data snapshot. The script wrote null values into required columns for 200 legacy subscriptions (pre‑2025) that lacked a plan_change_date field, corrupting those records in the snapshot.
- Impact: 200 subscription records in the snapshot were corrupted. No customer‑facing impact (this was a snapshot), but the run required 4 hours to identify and restore affected records and triggered an executive question. We lost engineering time, exposed us to a high risk if the same run had targeted production, and damaged confidence.
- Key numbers: 200 records affected; monitoring alerted in 12 minutes (alert threshold 15 minutes); full restore completed in ~4 hours; customer‑facing comms template sent within 30 minutes (prepared though no customers were impacted).

Timeline (summary)
- 09:00 — Alan started dry‑run against production snapshot.
- 09:12 — Sarah’s monitoring alert fired (12 minutes).
- 09:15 — Alan confirmed corruption and stopped the script.
- 09:30 — Josh sent the pre‑written customer/exec communication.
- 13:00 — All corrupted records restored from backup; root cause identified.

Root cause
- Primary cause: Migration script assumed every subscription had a plan_change_date field. ~200 legacy subscriptions (created before 2025) do not have this field. The script wrote nulls into required columns, causing data integrity issues.
- Contributing factors:
  - No preflight validation in the migration script to detect missing required fields before writing.
  - Dry‑run was executed in a way that allowed writes to the production snapshot rather than against a read‑only copy or isolated environment.
  - Insufficient test coverage: migration was not tested against production‑scale historical data that included legacy accounts with missing fields.
  - Run checklist/runbook and approval process did not force the above validations or require a canary subset.

What went right
- Monitoring detected anomalies quickly (12 minutes) — under our 15‑minute threshold.
- Alan’s rollback script (built last month) worked and allowed a full restore.
- Pre‑written communications existed, enabling timely internal/executive notification.
- Team responded quickly and contained the issue before any customer impact.

Why this was risky
- Although this run touched a snapshot, the same script run against production would have produced incorrect plan tiers and billing amounts for customers — potentially billing errors, refunds, legal exposure, and major customer support load.
- The presence of 200 legacy records with missing fields suggests other legacy data gaps may exist; other migrations could surface similar failures.

Action items (owner — due date — verification)
Immediate (within 48 hours)
1) Make dry‑run default non‑destructive: Add an enforced "read‑only/dry_run" flag defaulting to true; migration must be opt‑in to write mode after explicit approval. — Owner: Alan — Due: 48 hours — Verify: Pull request merged; attempt dry‑run against snapshot shows no writes.
2) Update migration runbook and require a migration approval checklist (preflight checks run, backups confirmed, approval from PM + Eng lead). No migration against prod snapshot without checklist sign‑off. — Owner: PM (Josh) — Due: 48 hours — Verify: Checklist document published; next migration blocked until signed.

Short term (3–7 days)
3) Add automated preflight checks to the migration script: validate required fields exist for target records, compare pre/post counts, fail fast if anomalies. — Owner: Alan & Sarah — Due: 3 days — Verify: Preflight test added; CI step fails if missing fields found.
4) Create an isolated, non‑writable copy of the prod snapshot for dry‑runs (or enforce read‑only mounts). Stop running writable operations on snapshots mapped to production. — Owner: Infra/Alan — Due: 3 days — Verify: Snapshot copy process documented and used for next dry‑run.
5) Audit legacy data for other missing required fields and generate a list of common gaps. — Owner: Sarah — Due: 7 days — Verify: Audit report with counts for missing fields.

Medium term (1–4 weeks)
6) Add production‑scale migration tests to CI that include legacy records (run in a gated environment). — Owner: Sarah — Due: 1 week — Verify: CI job runs nightly; migration tests pass.
7) Implement canary process: run migrations on a small percentage of records first and validate, before full rollout. — Owner: Alan — Due: 2 weeks — Verify: Canary process documented and used on next migration.
8) Backfill plan_change_date for legacy accounts where appropriate OR make migration handle missing plan_change_date safely (business logic to default or reject). Propose backfill vs. safe handling after data audit. — Owner: Alan — Due: proposal in 2 weeks, implementation in 4 weeks — Verify: Backfill script and tests; canary validation.

Longer term (4–8 weeks)
9) Review schema & model assumptions: decide whether plan_change_date should be required going forward, and if so, schedule a safe schema migration + backfill plan. — Owner: PM + Alan — Due: 6 weeks — Verify: Decision & migration plan approved.
10) Incorporate migration run playbook into on‑call and incident training: simulate a migration failure scenario in next ops drill. — Owner: PM (Josh) — Due: 8 weeks — Verify: Drill executed and retrospective documented.

Risk remaining
- There may be other legacy data anomalies not yet uncovered. The audit (action 5) must complete before we run any similar migration on production.
- Until the preflight checks and non‑writable snapshot practice are in place, running any migration against production snapshots remains a high risk. We must enforce the checklist immediately.

Postmortem acceptance criteria
- Preflight checks in place and used.
- Non‑writable snapshot environment established and used for dry‑runs.
- Audit of legacy data completed and any required backfills or safe handling implemented.
- Migration runbook updated and sign‑off enforced.

Owner confirmations requested
- Alan: confirm timeline for code changes (items 1, 3, 7, 8) and that rollback remains tested.
- Sarah: confirm audit schedule and CI test additions (items 3, 5, 6).
- Josh/PM: confirm runbook checklist and enforcement (items 2, 10).

Closing / lessons learned
- We got lucky: monitoring and the rollback script prevented customer impact. This was a near‑miss with clear, preventable failure modes.
- Core lesson: migrations must assume messy legacy data. Assume absence of fields; validate before writing. Dry‑runs must be non‑destructive by default and executed against isolated read‑only environments.
- Accountability: Alan led the run and owns the migration fixes; Sarah owns monitoring and the data audit; Josh/PM will own the runbook and approvals. We will treat this as a high priority: fixes and process updates begin immediately.

If you want, I’ll:
- Open the action‑item tickets with owners and due dates and add them to the sprint board.
- Schedule a 30‑minute retro with Alan, Sarah, and Josh to walk the timeline and confirm the runbook changes.

Recommend we start with the two 48‑hour items (default non‑destructive dry‑run + runbook checklist). They reduce the immediate risk of any future runs. Which do you want me to create first as tickets?

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text:
Short answer: I cannot sign off on the capacity plan as-is.

Why not (numbers first)
- Stripe plan = 100 req/sec.
- Planned steady peaks: Billing 60 req/sec + API v2 35 req/sec = 95 req/sec → 95% of limit.
- Typical traffic spikes = 1.5–2x → combined = 143–190 req/sec → well above 100 → will produce 429s.
- Billing runs in a concentrated window (1am–3am PST). That makes collisions likely and high-impact (invoice generation + on-demand reconciliation).
- No shared client/caching layer exists, so both teams will independently flood Stripe.

Concrete risks
- Customer-facing failures (subscription lookups) and invoice processing failures from 429s during the billing window or any spike.
- High retry traffic increasing load and amplifying the spike (retry storms).
- Lack of observability/controls: we don’t currently have a plan to detect and mitigate 429s in production.
- Shipping both next week without mitigations is high probability of outage and immediate customer impact — we have one shot to ship.

What I recommend now (priority-ordered, actionable, with owners & timeline)
1) Immediate short-term throttle + graceful degradation (0–48 hours)
   - Owner: Sarah (API v2) + Alan (Billing)
   - Implement a client-side rate limiter (leaky-bucket/token-bucket) per service that caps calls to Stripe so combined steady-state stays ≤100 req/sec. Practically: limit API v2 to 20–25 req/sec during billing window and/or limit billing concurrency. Timeline: 48 hours.
   - Add exponential backoff with jitter and a circuit breaker that returns cached/stale data or 503 to clients when Stripe is rate-limited. Timeline: 48 hours.

2) Fast wins to reduce Stripe traffic (48–96 hours)
   - Owner: Sarah — add caching for subscription lookups (TTL 30s–5m, tuned by traffic).
   - Owner: Alan — stop polling for payment confirmation; switch to webhooks where possible, or drastically reduce polling frequency and stagger reconciliation jobs outside the 1am–3am peak.

3) Coordination of billing window (48–72 hours)
   - Owner: PM to coordinate.
   - Options: stagger invoice generation across customers or limit API v2 heavy work during 1am–3am PST (apply temporary schedule-based throttles).
   - Why: reduces concentrated bursts that push us over the limit.

4) Shared client / connection pooling (next week)
   - Owner: Engineering (Alan + Sarah)
   - Build a shared Stripe client or proxy that enforces global concurrency limits and caches common data. This centralizes rate limiting and pooling.

5) Longer-term / if we need room for spikes (business decision, ASAP)
   - Option A: Upgrade Stripe plan to a higher rate limit (e.g., 200 req/sec). Owner: Finance/Product; timeline depends on Stripe.
   - Option B: Combine caching + shared client + rate limiting and accept current plan if load tests show headroom.
   - I’ll quantify cost vs risk if you want me to pursue upgrade quotes.

6) Observability and load testing (must do before ship)
   - Owner: Eng + PM
   - Add Stripe request rate, 429/error rate, and per-service breakdown to dashboards/alerts.
   - Run an end-to-end load test that simulates 1.5x and 2x traffic and the 1am–3am billing window, and show 5-minute max requests to Stripe ≤100 for the “safe” configuration.
   - Timeline: run within 72–96 hours.

Go/no-go criteria for sign-off
- One of these must be true before I sign off:
  1) We upgrade Stripe rate limit to cover 2x spikes (e.g., >=190 req/sec), OR
  2) We implement and validate (via load test) a mitigation bundle that demonstrably keeps observed Stripe requests ≤100 req/sec during a simulated 2x spike. Mitigation bundle = client-side/global rate limiting + caching + circuit breaker + monitoring + policy to avoid heavy billing-window traffic.
- And in both cases: dashboards/alerts for Stripe 429s in place.

If you want a single immediate checklist to act on today
- Add per-service simple rate limiter to cap calls to Stripe (temporary).
- Implement a short cache for subscription lookups (5 min TTL to start).
- Switch billing confirmation from polling → webhooks or reduce polling frequency and stagger jobs.
- Add Stripe 429 metric and an alert (>=1% 429s over 1m).
- Run a quick load test simulating 1.5x and 2x.

I’ll coordinate owners and timeline if you want — recommend we block shipping both changes next week until at least the immediate throttles + caching + load test pass. Without that, we accept a high chance of customer-impacting 429s during the billing window.
